taxonomy.title: UI Density / Input Assumption / Chrome / Visual Language
taxonomy.description: A practical UI classification modeled as independent axes (dimensions). Each UI can be described as a profile across axes, plus optional measured metrics.

axes.title: Density
axes.description: How much space is used per control; how much content fits on screen.

levels.label: Compact
levels.notes: Low padding; more items visible; tighter line height; smaller minimum control sizes.

levels.label: Comfortable
levels.notes: Balanced spacing; readable; moderate minimum control sizes.

levels.label: Spacious
levels.notes: Generous padding; fewer items visible; larger minimum control sizes.

axes.title: Input Assumption
axes.description: What the UI seems optimized for: keyboard/pointer precision vs touch-first targets.

levels.label: Keyboard/Pointer-first
levels.notes: Precision targets okay; hover is useful; shortcuts common; dense layouts acceptable.

levels.label: Mixed
levels.notes: Works well with pointer and touch; targets moderate; less reliance on hover.

levels.label: Touch-first
levels.notes: Large hit targets; extra spacing; simplified navigation; minimal reliance on hover.

axes.title: Chrome Level
axes.description: How much visible UI framing exists around content (toolbars, borders, persistent panels, window decorations).

levels.label: Low-chrome
levels.notes: Content dominates; fewer persistent bars/panels; minimal decoration.

levels.label: Medium-chrome
levels.notes: Some persistent chrome; common desktop app layout patterns.

levels.label: High-chrome
levels.notes: More framing UI; multiple toolbars/panels; stronger window/controls presence.

axes.title: Visual Language
axes.description: Primarily aesthetic rendering style. Optional but useful to describe 'Motif/classic vs modern'.

levels.label: Beveled/3D
levels.notes: Raised/sunken controls, strong borders, classic widget framing.

levels.label: Flat/Minimal
levels.notes: Few borders; color/spacing defines structure; minimal decoration.

levels.label: Card/Elevation
levels.notes: Surfaces/cards; shadows/elevation cues; spaced layouts common.

metrics.title: Average control padding (px)
metrics.notes: Average of horizontal/vertical padding across primary buttons/inputs/labels.

metrics.title: Minimum interactive target size (px)
metrics.notes: Smallest tappable/clickable target (height or min of width/height) used in common flows.

metrics.title: Line height ratio
metrics.notes: Computed as line-height / font-size for body text.

metrics.title: Items visible in a standard list view
metrics.notes: How many list rows are visible at 100% scaling in a reference window size.

metrics.title: Chrome area ratio
metrics.notes: Estimated (chrome pixels) / (window pixels). Chrome = titlebar + toolbars + persistent sidebars/headers/footers.

metrics.title: Hover dependency
metrics.notes: How much essential functionality is hidden behind hover-only interactions.

scoring_guidance.purpose: Provide a consistent way to derive axis scores from metrics when you want automation.

heuristics.example_rule_of_thumb: More padding + larger line height + fewer visible list items => higher density score (more spacious).
Larger minimum targets + low hover dependency => higher touch-first score.

example_profiles.name: Dense Minimal Desktop
example_profiles.notes: Typical of tiling/WMs and very compact app themes.

example_profiles.name: Classic Framed Desktop
example_profiles.notes: Strong widget framing and visible chrome; medium spacing.

example_profiles.name: Touch-Spacious Modern
example_profiles.notes: Large hit targets and generous spacing; cards/elevation often used.

ui_instance_template.notes: Optional notes about why scores/levels were chosen.

sources.details: Reviewer observations and screenshots reference IDs (if any).
